name: Martynova et al. Teacher-Observed LLM Student Authenticity Extract
population_or_context: Teachers and tutors of STEM subjects who had tutored LLM-simulated sixth-grade students in MathDial.
sample_size: 12 teacher/tutor interviews; MathDial context includes 2,861 dialogues from 90 teacher participants and 377 optional teacher feedback entries.
methods: The study analyzes teacher ratings and feedback in MathDial, then conducts semi-structured interviews with teachers who interacted with LLM-simulated students.
education_context: Teacher training and lesson-preparation simulation using LLM-simulated math students.
response_origin: MathDial LLM-simulated student dialogues generated with GPT-3.5-turbo, followed by teacher/tutor interaction and rating.
rubric_label_space: Teacher ratings and qualitative authenticity judgments rather than automated scoring labels.
label_source: Teacher ratings, teacher feedback, and interview analysis grounded in Community of Inquiry and Scaffolding frameworks.
generation_model: GPT-3.5-turbo for the MathDial simulated students analyzed in the paper.
generation_method: LLM was prompted to generate an initial incorrect solution and act as a student who believes the solution is correct, with persona/confusion details.
prompt_strategy: Student persona plus initial incorrect solution and specified confusion from MathDial.
evaluation_method: LDA topic modeling of teacher feedback, statistical comparison of typical versus non-typical interactions, and semi-structured teacher interviews.
scorer_use: Student-simulation authenticity evaluation; not automated scorer validation.
real_response_comparator: Teachers compare LLM-simulated student behavior with their experience of real students; the paper also contrasts typical and non-typical teacher-rated simulated interactions.
reported_metrics: Teachers rated 21% of MathDial conversations as non-typical and 79% as typical; 51 of 377 feedback entries mentioned repetitive answers; 8 of 12 interviewees noted limited emotional response; 10 of 12 reported high attentiveness.
limitations: The simulated-student dataset used GPT-3.5-turbo, so results should not be treated as a current model ranking.
The evidence concerns teacher perceptions and dialogue authenticity, not scoring-model validity.
The sample is small and centered on teachers/tutors with prior MathDial participation.
